body_txt: In fictional media, whenever an assassin or Ninja are introduced, they are usually accompanied by a mystical power. This could be inspired by Japanese folklore, which would feature ninjas, with legendary abilities, such as walking on water , Invisibility , and control over natural elements . The Trope Codifier is probably author Futaro Yamada (1922-2001), who established the idea of "ninja skills as superpowers". Since Ninjas have their own magic and spells, in most media, it is called Ninjutsu, which is named after the martial art. One quirk of this is that elemental techniques will generally be named with the character 遁 ( ton , "Escape/Release"), a relic of their more mundane origins; e.g. a historical 火遁の術 ( Katon no Jutsu , "Fire Escape Technique") was at most using fire or smoke to distract guards ( possibly via specially-crafted arrows or bombs ), but fictional katon is upgraded to straight-out Playing with Fire abilities. A hallmark of the Wuxia genre. This is a subtrope of Functional Magic . Compare with All Asians Know Martial Arts , Everybody Was Kung-Fu Fighting , I Know Karate , and Spell Construction . See also Magic Knight for a warrior who uses weapons and spells together, and Psychic Operative for when the ninjutsu techniques are hypnotic or mind-affecting. Tropes associated are Ki Manipulation , Supernatural Martial Arts , Kung-Fu Wizard and Ninja Log . See also Ninja Pirate Zombie Robot .
